perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees not to disclose or reveal any Confidential Information , including the existence of the Listing, to any persons or entities other than Buyer’s employees or representatives who are directly participating in the evaluation of th e information , and only then in connection with the proposed acquisition.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees to use reasonable efforts not to disclose or reveal Confidential Information, including the existence of the Listing, to any persons or entities, especially concerning Buyer’s employees or representatives participating in the evaluation of information in connection with the proposed acquisition.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees not to disclose' to 'agrees to use reasonable efforts not to disclose' introduces ambiguity. 'Reasonable efforts' is subjective and doesn't clearly define the required level of confidentiality. Further, adding 'especially concerning Buyer's employees or representatives' adds another layer of ambiguity as to what information is or isn't covered.
perturbation.law_explanation: MGL c. 93A, § 11 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. By weakening the obligation to maintain confidentiality with the term 'reasonable efforts,' the modification could facilitate the misuse of confidential information, potentially constituting an unfair or deceptive practice if it harms the broker or listing party. This contradicts the spirit and intent of MGL c. 93A, § 11.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity by changing 'agrees not to disclose' to 'agrees to use reasonable efforts not to disclose'. This weakening of the confidentiality obligation can be interpreted as an 'unfair or deceptive act or practice' under MGL c. 93A, § 11, as it could facilitate the misuse of confidential information, potentially harming the broker or listing party. The phrase 'reasonable efforts' does not provide a clear standard, making enforcement difficult. Both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 emphasize that 'unfair or deceptive act or practice' is unlawful. The change directly contradicts the intent to prevent unfair trade practices by weakening the obligation to protect confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer shall not directly contact th e Listing owners or their landlords, employees, agents, representatives, suppliers or customers (collectively, the “Listing Party”) except through Broker. All correspondence, inquiries , offers to purchase and negotiations relating to the purchase or lease of any Listing presented by Broker will be conducted exclusively through Broker.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer should generally avoid directly contacting the Listing Party except possibly not through Broker. Correspondence, inquiries, offers to purchase, and negotiations related to the Listing may be conducted without the Broker's explicit consent.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'should generally avoid' introduces ambiguity and weakens the restriction on direct contact. Adding 'except possibly not through Broker' creates a double negative, making the obligation unclear. Furthermore, stating correspondence 'may be conducted without the Broker's explicit consent' directly contradicts the original intent of exclusive dealings.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing the buyer to sidestep the broker, the revised clause potentially facilitates unfair competition and deceptive practices, violating MGL c. 93A, § 2. This law prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in any trade or commerce.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The altered contract weakens the original intent of exclusive dealings with the broker, potentially leading to unfair competition. The original text strictly prohibits direct contact with the 'Listing Party' except through the broker, while the modified text introduces ambiguity by stating the buyer 'should generally avoid' direct contact and that correspondence 'may be conducted without the Broker's explicit consent.' This contradicts the original exclusive dealing arrangement. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, MGL c. 93A, § 2(a) declares 'unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful.' Allowing the buyer to bypass the broker and potentially engage in direct negotiations could be viewed as an unfair practice, undermining the broker's role and potentially creating an unfair competitive advantage for the buyer.

perturbation.original_text: The Agreement and all rights and obligations hereunder, including matters of construction, validity and performance shall be governe d by the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts. All parties submit themselves to the jurisdiction of the courts of Massachusetts, for all purposes with respect to the Agre ement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement and some rights and obligations hereunder, including construction matters, may be governed loosely by the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts. All parties, to a degree, submit to the courts of Massachusetts with regard to the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'all rights and obligations' to 'some rights and obligations' and 'shall be governed' to 'may be governed loosely' introduces significant uncertainty regarding which aspects of the agreement are subject to Massachusetts law. Adding 'to a degree' further weakens the commitment to Massachusetts jurisdiction. This undermines the principle of clear and predictable legal jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: By creating uncertainty around which laws govern the agreement, the ambiguous clause potentially weakens the protections afforded by MGL c. 93A, § 11. A core principle of Chapter 93A is to ensure fair dealing in commerce. When the governing law is uncertain, parties might be able to evade liability for unfair or deceptive practices that would otherwise be actionable under Massachusetts law. The phrase 'may be governed loosely' suggests that some aspects of unfair trade practices are not held to the full scrutiny of the law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding which aspects of the agreement are subject to Massachusetts law and weakens the commitment to Massachusetts jurisdiction. This directly contradicts the principle of ensuring fair dealing in commerce, as uncertainty around the governing law could allow parties to evade liability for unfair or deceptive practices, which would be actionable under MGL c. 93A, § 11. Both Snippet 1 and Snippet 2 explicitly mention actions for unfair trade practices. By making the application of Massachusetts law uncertain ('may be governed loosely'), the modified contract undermines the protections afforded by Chapter 93A, creating a direct contradiction. The change removes a legally mandated protection.
